claim_text: In melanoma, anti-CTLA-4 treatment reinvigorates exhausted PD-1+Eomes+CD8 T cells.

runs.response_text:
VERDICT: SUPPORT

The abstract titled "Radiation and Dual Checkpoint Blockade Activates Non-Redundant Immune Mechanisms in Cancer" provides direct evidence supporting the claim. It states that "optimal response in melanoma and other cancer types requires radiation, anti-CTLA-4 and anti-PD-L1;" and that "Anti-CTLA4 predominantly inhibits T-regulatory cells (Treg cells), thereby increasing the CD8 T-cell to Treg (CD8/Treg) ratio. Radiation enhances the diversity of the T-cell receptor (TCR) repertoire of intratumoral T cells. Together, anti-CTLA4 promotes expansion of T cells, while radiation shapes the TCR repertoire of the expanded peripheral clones. Addition of PD-L1 blockade reverses T-cell exhaustion to mitigate depression in the CD8/Treg ratio and further encourages oligoclonal T-cell expansion." This indicates that anti-CTLA-4 treatment, in conjunction with

VERDICT: CONTRADICT
The abstracts do not provide evidence that anti-CTLA-4 treatment reinvigorates exhausted PD-1+Eomes+CD8 T cells in melanoma. The abstracts discuss the role of anti-CTLA-4 in promoting T-cell exhaustion and the need for combination therapies with anti-PD-L1/PD-1 to achieve optimal responses. There is no mention of reinvigorating exhausted T cells in the context of melanoma or any other cancer type.

VERDICT: CONTRADICT

The abstract explicitly states that "Patients with melanoma showing high PD-L1 did not respond to radiation plus anti-CTLA-4, demonstrated persistent T-cell exhaustion, and rapidly progressed." This indicates that anti-CTLA-4 alone does not reinvigorate exhausted PD-1⁺Eomes⁺CD8 T cells in the presence of high PD-L1 expression on melanoma cells, directly contradicting the claim.

VERDICT: CONTRADICT
The abstracts do not provide evidence that anti-CTLA-4 treatment reinvigorates exhausted PD-1+Eomes+CD8 T cells in melanoma. Instead, the abstracts suggest that anti-CTLA4 treatment increases T-cell exhaustion by promoting Treg cell expansion and upregulating PD-L1 on melanoma cells, which hinders T-cell responses.

VERDICT: SUPPORT

The abstract for [doc_id 4468861] directly supports the claim. It reports that combined treatment with radiation and anti-CTLA-4 in mouse models and patients with metastatic melanoma results in major tumor regressions. The abstract specifically notes that this effect is mediated by the reversal of T-cell exhaustion through PD-L1 upregulation and associated with the PD-1+ Eomes+ CD8 T cell population, indicating that anti-CTLA-4 treatment reinvigorates these exhausted T cells in melanoma.

VERDICT: CONTRADICT
The abstracts do not provide evidence that anti-CTLA-4 treatment reinvigorates exhausted PD-1+Eomes+CD8 T cells in melanoma. Instead, the abstracts suggest that anti-CTLA4 treatment promotes expansion of T cells, enhances the diversity of the TCR repertoire, and reverses T-cell exhaustion by increasing the CD8/Treg ratio and encouraging oligoclonal T-cell expansion. These findings contradict the claim that anti-CTLA-4 treatment reinvigorates exhausted PD-1+Eomes+CD8 T cells.
